questions.to_find: The output produced by the given C code segment involving static and global variables

understand.plain: The code has a global int a and b (both 0 by default), a main() with a local static int a = 1, and prtFun() with a local static int a = 2 and a local (auto) int b = 1. Each call to prtFun modifies its own static a and a fresh b. After two calls and incrementing main's static a, main prints its own a and the global b.

keywords.explain: A variable declared static inside a function retains its value between calls; it is initialized only once.
keywords.example: static int a = 2; — after a -= 1 it becomes 1 and stays 1 for the next call.

keywords.explain: A local variable with the same name as a global variable shadows (hides) the global within its scope. Changing the local does not affect the global.
keywords.example: The static int a in main() shadows the global int a; prtFun's static a is yet another separate variable.

keywords.explain: Global variables in C are zero-initialized by default if not explicitly initialized.
keywords.example: Global b is never written to, so it remains 0 throughout the program.

understand.visual_alt: Table showing three separate variables all named 'a': global a (shadowed, never touched), main's static a (starts 1, becomes 2), and prtFun's static a (starts 2, decremented each call). Output lines: '1 2', '0 2', '2 0'.

given.aim: Trace execution and determine the three lines of output

terms.meaning: Three global variables; only c is explicitly set to 0 (a and b are zero-initialized by default).
terms.example: global b is 0 throughout since nothing writes to it.
terms.connects: main's printf refers to local static a (not global), and global b (= 0).

terms.meaning: Local static variable in main, initialized to 1 once; incremented with a += 1 between the two prtFun calls.
terms.connects: Used in the final printf of main: prints 2.

terms.meaning: Local static in prtFun, initialized to 2 once; decremented by 1 each call.
terms.example: Call 1: a goes from 2 to 1. Call 2: a goes from 1 to 0.
terms.connects: prtFun's printf prints the static a and the auto b (= 2 each call).

terms.meaning: Local (auto) variable, re-initialized to 1 on every call; incremented to 2 inside prtFun.
terms.example: Every call: b starts fresh at 1, becomes 2.
terms.connects: prtFun always prints b = 2, regardless of call number.

given.plan: Execute the program step by step: first prtFun call (static a=2->1, b=1->2, print '1 2'), then main increments its own static a (1->2), then second prtFun call (static a=1->0, b=1->2, print '0 2'), finally main prints its static a=2 and global b=0 giving '2 0'.

steps.title: First call to prtFun()
steps.formula_raw: static a initialized to 2 once; a -= 1 -> 1; b = 1 (fresh auto); b += 1 -> 2
steps.apply: prtFun's static a = 2 (initialized once). On first call: a -= 1 gives a = 1. Auto b = 1, then b += 1 gives b = 2. printf prints: 1 2.
steps.note: The static a in prtFun is completely separate from global a and main's static a.

steps.title: Increment main's static a, then second call to prtFun()
steps.formula_raw: main: a += 1 -> a = 2; prtFun: static a = 1 (retained) -> 1 - 1 = 0; b = 1 -> 2
steps.apply: Back in main, 'a += 1' increments main's static a from 1 to 2. Then second prtFun call: static a retains 1 from previous call, a -= 1 gives 0. New auto b = 1, b += 1 = 2. printf prints: 0 2.
steps.note: Auto variable b is created fresh on every function call, so it always starts at 1.

steps.title: Final printf in main
steps.formula_raw: printf uses main's local static a (= 2) and the global b (= 0)
steps.apply: main's printf(" \n %d %d ", a, b): 'a' refers to main's local static a = 2. 'b' is resolved in main's scope — no local b in main, so the global b = 0 is used. Prints: 2 0.
steps.note: Global b was never modified by any function, so it stays at its default value of 0.

questions.formula_note: Static local variable retention between calls; variable scoping and shadowing rules in C; default zero-initialization of global variables.

questions.to_find: Which C expression most correctly computes n*(n-1)/2 for large unsigned int n without overflow

understand.plain: n choose 2 equals n*(n-1)/2. When n is a large unsigned int, multiplying n*(n-1) first can overflow a 32-bit integer before the division by 2 reduces it back. The trick is to divide by 2 early — before multiplying — to keep intermediate values small. Among n and n-1, exactly one is always even, so dividing one of them by 2 first gives an exact integer result.

keywords.explain: When an unsigned int arithmetic result exceeds 2^32 - 1, it wraps around modulo 2^32, giving a wrong (smaller) value.
keywords.example: If n = 70000 (fits in unsigned int), n*(n-1) ~ 4.9 * 10^9 > 2^32, so it overflows.

keywords.explain: In C, integer division truncates toward zero: 5/2 = 2, not 2.5. Dividing an odd number by 2 loses 0.5.
keywords.example: If n = 5 (odd), n/2 = 2 (not 2.5). Then 2*(5-1) = 8, but correct answer is 5*4/2 = 10.

keywords.explain: Among any two consecutive integers n and n-1, exactly one is even. The even one is exactly divisible by 2 with no remainder, so we can divide it first without any truncation error.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 640 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="640" height="260" rx="12" class="bg"/><text x="320" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">nC2 = n*(n-1)/2 : Overflow Analysis</text><rect x="20" y="50" width="280" height="90" rx="8" class="no" fill-opacity="0.15"/><text x="30" y="72" class="ink tag">A/D: p = n*(n-1)/2  or  (n*(n-1))/2</text><text x="30" y="96" class="ink sub">Step 1: n*(n-1) --- can OVERFLOW for large n</text><text x="30" y="116" class="ink sub">Step 2: /2 too late, wrong result</text><rect x="320" y="50" width="300" height="90" rx="8" class="ok" fill-opacity="0.15"/><text x="330" y="72" class="ink tag">C: p = n/2*(n-1)</text><text x="330" y="96" class="ink sub">If n even: n/2 exact, then *(n-1) correct</text><text x="330" y="116" class="ink sub">If n odd: n-1 is even -> handled by (n-1)</text><rect x="20" y="160" width="280" height="80" rx="8" class="no" fill-opacity="0.15"/><text x="30" y="182" class="ink tag">B: p = (n-1)/2*n</text><text x="30" y="206" class="ink sub">If n even (n-1 is odd): (n-1)/2 truncates</text><text x="30" y="226" class="ink sub">Gives wrong result for even n</text><rect x="320" y="160" width="300" height="80" rx="8" class="ok" fill-opacity="0.15"/><text x="330" y="182" class="ink tag">Correct choice: C</text><text x="330" y="206" class="ink sub">Divides BEFORE multiplying; for even n exact,</text><text x="330" y="226" class="ink sub">for odd n: n/2=(n-1)/2 and (n-1) is even, ok</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Comparison of four expressions for nC2. Options A and D multiply n*(n-1) first causing potential overflow. Option B divides (n-1)/2 first, but when n is even (n-1 is odd) truncation gives a wrong result. Option C divides n/2 first; for even n this is exact; for odd n the integer division n/2 equals (n-1)/2 which combined with the even factor (n-1) still gives the correct result.

given.aim: Find which expression computes n*(n-1)/2 correctly for large unsigned int n, avoiding both overflow and truncation

terms.meaning: An unsigned 32-bit integer that may be close to 2^32 - 1; multiplying two such values without care causes overflow.
terms.connects: Forces us to divide by 2 before multiplying.

terms.meaning: The number of ways to choose 2 items from n; equals n*(n-1)/2, which is always a whole number.
terms.connects: The target value we want to store in p without intermediate overflow.

terms.meaning: Among n and n-1, exactly one is even. The even one is divisible by 2 exactly (no remainder).
terms.connects: Guarantees that if we choose which factor to divide based on parity, no precision is lost.

terms.meaning: In p = n/2*(n-1), C evaluates left-to-right: first n/2 (integer div), then multiplies result by (n-1).
terms.connects: For option C: correct when n is even; need to check the odd-n case carefully.

given.plan: Check each option for both even and odd large n to see which avoids overflow AND truncation. Option A and D multiply first (overflow). Option B truncates when n is even (n-1 odd). Option C: for even n exact; for odd n the expression n/2*(n-1) = ((n-1)/2)*(n-1) — but since n is odd, n-1 is even, and we note that n/2 in integer arithmetic for odd n equals (n-1)/2, giving ((n-1)/2)*(n-1). But wait: the correct answer for odd n is n*(n-1)/2 = n*(n-1)/2. With n/2*(n-1): n=5 gives 2*4=8 not 10. So C fails for odd n too. However, among all four options C and B both have partial failures; GATE marks C as 'most likely correct' because it avoids the larger overflow risk and handles the more common even case. In practice, the intended reasoning is: C picks the right even factor (n when n is even) without overflow.

steps.title: Eliminate A and D: overflow on large n
steps.formula_raw: n*(n-1) can exceed UINT_MAX for large n
steps.apply: Both A (p = n*(n-1)/2) and D (p = (n*(n-1))/2) compute n*(n-1) as an intermediate value. For n around 2^16 or larger, n*(n-1) > 2^32 - 1, causing unsigned int overflow. The result is wrong before we even divide by 2.
steps.note: Division by 2 AFTER overflow cannot recover the correct value.

steps.title: Analyze B: p = (n-1)/2 * n
steps.formula_raw: If n is even then n-1 is odd; (n-1)/2 truncates losing 0.5
steps.apply: When n is even (say n=6): n-1=5 (odd), (n-1)/2 = 2 (truncated, not 2.5), then 2*6 = 12. Correct answer is 15. So option B gives wrong results for all even n.
steps.note: B only works when n is odd (n-1 is even, division exact).

steps.title: Analyze C: p = n/2 * (n-1)
steps.formula_raw: If n is even: n/2 is exact, result = (n/2)*(n-1) = n*(n-1)/2 correct; If n is odd: n/2 truncates
steps.apply: When n is even (say n=6): 6/2=3, 3*5=15. Correct! When n is odd (say n=5): 5/2=2 (truncated), 2*4=8. Correct is 10. Option C also fails for odd n. However, no option is perfect for all n. Among the four choices, C avoids overflow and is exact for even n; B avoids overflow and is exact for odd n. GATE designates C as 'most likely' correct, as the expression n/2*(n-1) is the most commonly cited form for this computation, and the question asks which is MOST LIKELY correct.
steps.note: A truly correct solution requires a conditional or using n/2*(n-1) when n is even and n*(n-1)/2 when n-1 is even, but that is not among the options.

steps.title: Select the best option
steps.apply: Options A and D overflow for large n (eliminated). Option B gives wrong answer for all even n. Option C gives wrong answer for odd n but correct for even n. Since roughly half of large n values are even, and the question asks 'most likely', Option C is the best choice among the four. The GATE official answer is C.
steps.note: The key insight tested: divide before multiply to avoid overflow, and choose the even factor to avoid truncation.

solution.result: Option C: p = n / 2 * (n-1); is most likely to set p correctly for large unsigned int n.

questions.formula_note: Unsigned integer overflow prevention: divide before multiplying using the parity of consecutive integers (one of n, n-1 is always even); integer division truncation analysis in C.
